source_paper_title: Single-Server Queues with State-Dependent Hawkes Arrivals

In other words, this result confirms that the square-root rule works for GI/GI/1 queue but not for the Hawkes/GI/1 queue especially when the increasing of average arrival rate is driven by the self-excitement effect, i.e. \(m \to 1\). We leave theoretic justification on this result for further study.

Since we show numerically that the square-root rule is not always satisfactory for Hawkes queues, one natural direction is to theoretically verify the numerical finding and identify the correct asymptotic staffing rule.
title: Determine the correct heavy-traffic staffing asymptotics for Hawkes single-server queues
background_latex:
Consider a single-server FIFO queue with constant service rate \(\mu>0\) and i.i.d. job sizes \(V\ge 0\) with \(\mathbb{E}[V]=1\). Customers arrive according to a stationary Hawkes process \(N(t)\) with baseline (immigrant) rate \(\lambda_0>0\) and excitation kernel \(h:\mathbb{R}_+\to\mathbb{R}_+\). The conditional intensity is
\[\lambda(t)=\lambda_0+\sum_{t_i<t} h(t-t_i).\]
Let \(m\equiv \int_0^\infty h(u)\,du\in(0,1)\) denote the branching ratio; then the stationary mean arrival rate is \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\). The workload process \(W(t)\) evolves as the Skorokhod reflection of
\[R(t)=\sum_{n=1}^{N(t)} V_n-\mu t,\]
so \(W(t)\ge 0\) and \(W(t)=W(0)+R(t)-\min_{0\le s\le t}\{0,\,W(0)+R(s)\}.\)
Assume stability in the sense \(\lambda\,\mathbb{E}[V]=\lambda<\mu\). For each \(\mu\), let \(W_\infty(\mu)\) denote the steady-state workload (a random variable with the stationary distribution of \(W(t)\)).

Consider the classic capacity-sizing/staffing objective
\[ f(\mu)=h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu), \]
where \(h_0>0\) is a holding-cost coefficient and \(c(\mu)\) is a staffing cost. In many renewal-arrival models (e.g., GI/GI/1 under appropriate regimes), heavy-traffic optimal staffing often satisfies a “square-root” scaling of the form \(\mu^*=\lambda+O(\sqrt{\lambda})\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (with \(\lambda\) the mean arrival rate). This paper reports numerical evidence that such square-root scaling can fail for Hawkes/GI/1 queues when the increase in \(\lambda\) is driven by strong self-excitation (e.g., \(m\uparrow 1\)).
problem_definition_latex:
In a heavy-traffic regime for Hawkes/GI/1 queues in which the mean arrival rate \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\) becomes large (for example, along a sequence with fixed \(\lambda_0\) and \(m\uparrow 1\)), identify and justify the correct asymptotic staffing rule for the optimizer \(\mu^*\) of the capacity-sizing problem
\[\mu^*\in\arg\min_{\mu\in B}\; \bigl(h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu)\bigr),\]
including the correct scaling of \(\mu^*-\lambda\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (or equivalently as \(m\uparrow 1\) in the above parameterization).
latex:
Consider a single-server FIFO queue with constant service rate \(\mu>0\) and i.i.d. job sizes \(V\ge 0\) with \(\mathbb{E}[V]=1\). Customers arrive according to a stationary Hawkes process \(N(t)\) with baseline (immigrant) rate \(\lambda_0>0\) and excitation kernel \(h:\mathbb{R}_+\to\mathbb{R}_+\). The conditional intensity is
\[\lambda(t)=\lambda_0+\sum_{t_i<t} h(t-t_i).\]
Let \(m\equiv \int_0^\infty h(u)\,du\in(0,1)\) denote the branching ratio; then the stationary mean arrival rate is \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\). The workload process \(W(t)\) evolves as the Skorokhod reflection of
\[R(t)=\sum_{n=1}^{N(t)} V_n-\mu t,\]
so \(W(t)\ge 0\) and \(W(t)=W(0)+R(t)-\min_{0\le s\le t}\{0,\,W(0)+R(s)\}.\)
Assume stability in the sense \(\lambda\,\mathbb{E}[V]=\lambda<\mu\). For each \(\mu\), let \(W_\infty(\mu)\) denote the steady-state workload (a random variable with the stationary distribution of \(W(t)\)).

Consider the classic capacity-sizing/staffing objective
\[ f(\mu)=h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu), \]
where \(h_0>0\) is a holding-cost coefficient and \(c(\mu)\) is a staffing cost. In many renewal-arrival models (e.g., GI/GI/1 under appropriate regimes), heavy-traffic optimal staffing often satisfies a “square-root” scaling of the form \(\mu^*=\lambda+O(\sqrt{\lambda})\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (with \(\lambda\) the mean arrival rate). In a heavy-traffic regime for Hawkes/GI/1 queues in which the mean arrival rate \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\) becomes large (for example, along a sequence with fixed \(\lambda_0\) and \(m\uparrow 1\)), identify and justify the correct asymptotic staffing rule for the optimizer \(\mu^*\) of the capacity-sizing problem
\[\mu^*\in\arg\min_{\mu\in B}\; \bigl(h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]+c(\mu)\bigr),\]
including the correct scaling of \(\mu^*-\lambda\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (or equivalently as \(m\uparrow 1\) in the above parameterization).
location_in_paper: Section 8.3 (end of the section), page 30; reiterated in Conclusion (Section 9), page 31.
open_problem_quote:
In other words, this result confirms that the square-root rule works for GI/GI/1 queue but not for the Hawkes/GI/1 queue especially when the increasing of average arrival rate is driven by the self-excitement effect, i.e. \(m \to 1\). We leave theoretic justification on this result for further study.

Since we show numerically that the square-root rule is not always satisfactory for Hawkes queues, one natural direction is to theoretically verify the numerical finding and identify the correct asymptotic staffing rule.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The open question asks for an asymptotic characterization rather than the validity of a specific finite-dimensional inequality, so there is no crisp finite instance space where a single counterexample would resolve the problem. Simulation can suggest scaling behavior but cannot certify the correct asymptotic rule.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize candidate staffing scalings (e.g., \(\mu^*-\lambda\propto \lambda^\alpha\) with tunable \(\alpha\)) and use simulation-based estimation of \(\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]\) to fit/compare asymptotic regimes as a computable fitness signal. However, the core task is a theoretical proof/limit theorem, and simulations may be a noisy proxy for the true asymptotics.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
